metadata.title: The war on crypto — the enforcement actors: Gensler, Hinman, Clayton, Damian Williams, Warren, the privacy-tool prosecutions, Choke Point 2.0, and the 2025 reversal (NEUTRAL, graded)
metadata.status: roles, dates, cases, and the Hinman-payment facts are FACT (public record); 'who was right' is NOT adjudicated — each action is graded on a reasonable<->overreach spectrum; no finding of corruption is asserted against any individual
metadata.frame: The institution-level enforcement was already mapped (SEC -> Ripple/Coinbase/Kraken/Binance/ConsenSys/LBRY; SDNY -> Tornado Cash/Samourai; FDIC pause-letters). This block adds the PEOPLE behind it — the principals who drove crypto enforcement 2017-2025 AND the principals who reversed it in 2025 AND the defenders — because the corpus maps who-sits-where without imputing intent. The honest finding is a pendulum: an era of aggressive 'regulation by enforcement' (some of it catching real fraud, some of it contested overreach with a documented conflict at its center), followed by a near-total 2025 reversal. Both swings are documented; neither is endorsed.
metadata.discipline: Each action graded on its own merits, NOT by the actor's team. Real fraud (SBF/FTX) is graded reasonable; novel developer-liability and code-as-speech theories (Tornado Cash) are graded contested; the Hinman appearance-of-conflict is graded as a documented ethics question, NOT proven corruption. 'Whether reasonable or not' means BOTH are in scope and labeled.

the_enforcers.gary_gensler: SEC Chair Apr 2021 - Jan 2025 (resigned at the change of administration). Drove 'regulation by enforcement' — 100+ crypto actions; the public position that nearly all tokens except Bitcoin are securities; suits against Coinbase, Kraken, and Binance; refused to clearly state whether ETH is a security in Congressional testimony (the central ambiguity crypto firms cite as a fair-notice failure). Former MIT blockchain lecturer. grade: the anti-fraud actions reasonable; the 'regulate by lawsuit without rulemaking' approach contested (courts split — e.g., the Ripple programmatic-sales ruling went against the SEC).
the_enforcers.william_hinman: SEC Director, Division of Corporation Finance (2017-2020). His 14 Jun 2018 speech declared Bitcoin and ETH 'sufficiently decentralized' and therefore NOT securities — creating the BTC/ETH carve-out the entire market relied on. The 'Hinman documents' became central to SEC v. Ripple (Ripple argued selective treatment / lack of fair notice; a court ordered the docs unsealed). DOCUMENTED CONFLICT QUESTION: while at the SEC, Hinman continued to receive multi-million-dollar payments from his former law firm Simpson Thacher (a member of the Enterprise Ethereum Alliance), which watchdogs (Empower Oversight FOIA) and the SEC Inspector General flagged as an appearance-of-conflict. grade: the speech + the payments + the IG concern are FACT; actual corruption is NOT asserted (appearance-of-conflict only).
the_enforcers.jay_clayton: SEC Chair 2017 - Dec 2020. Filed SEC v. Ripple on 22 Dec 2020 — in the final days of his tenure. The irony the corpus notes: Clayton was later appointed US Attorney for the SDNY (2025) — the office that PROSECUTES crypto crime — so the man who launched the signature SEC crypto case now runs the signature crypto-prosecution office. grade: fact (roles, the Ripple filing, the SDNY appointment); the 'revolving-position' observation is structural, not an allegation.
the_enforcers.damian_williams: US Attorney, SDNY (2021-2025). Oversaw the marquee crypto prosecutions: SBF/FTX (real, adjudicated fraud — grade reasonable), Avraham Eisenberg/Mango Markets, and US v. Roman Storm (Tornado Cash) — where the unlicensed-money-transmitter theory against a privacy-protocol developer is contested (the jury convicted on transmitting but HUNG on the laundering/sanctions counts; see spec-sdny-crypto-prosecution). grade: mixed by case, as labeled.
the_enforcers.caroline_crenshaw: SEC Commissioner — the dissenting voice MOST aligned with aggressive crypto enforcement; dissented from the 2025 case dismissals. Her renomination stalled in the Senate (2025). grade: fact.
the_enforcers.elizabeth_warren: US Senator; organized the 'anti-crypto army' framing; lead sponsor of the Digital Asset Anti-Money-Laundering Act; a leading voice for the debanking posture (Operation Choke Point 2.0). grade: fact (the bills, the positions); 'right or wrong' not adjudicated.
the_enforcers.letitia_james: NY Attorney General; her office's KuCoin suit (2023) explicitly argued ETH IS a security — a state-level position in direct tension with Hinman's federal carve-out. grade: fact.
the_enforcers.rostin_behnam: CFTC Chair; argued for CFTC (commodity) jurisdiction over crypto — the inter-agency turf fight (SEC-security vs CFTC-commodity) that left firms without clear rules. grade: fact.

the_choke_point_layer: Operation Choke Point 2.0 (term coined by Nic Carter) — the alleged coordinated DEBANKING of crypto firms via FDIC 'pause letters', Fed/OCC guidance, and reputational-risk pressure (documented in spec-crypto-banking-debanking; the FDIC pause-letters surfaced via Coinbase FOIA litigation). Distinct from securities enforcement: it cut crypto off from BANKING rather than suing it. grade: the pause-letters are FACT (FOIA-released); 'coordinated operation' is contested framing.
the_reversal_2025: The pendulum swung hard in 2025: Paul Atkins became SEC Chair (pro-crypto); the SEC DROPPED or settled its cases against Coinbase, Ripple, Kraken, ConsenSys, Robinhood, Uniswap, OpenSea, and Gemini; Hester Peirce ('Crypto Mom', the long-time Gensler dissenter) was put in charge of a new SEC Crypto Task Force; DOJ's National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Team (NCET) was effectively disbanded (Apr 2025 Blanche memo: DOJ 'is not a digital-asset enforcer'); and the GENIUS Act + market-structure legislation moved (spec-crypto-legislation-forcing). grade: fact. The honest read: this is a regime reversal, not a vindication of either side — the SAME conduct went from prosecuted to permitted on a political clock.
the_defenders: The counter-side, for balance: John Deaton (filed amicus for ~75,000 XRP holders in SEC v. Ripple; ran against Warren in 2024, lost); Brad Garlinghouse (Ripple CEO, the public face of the multi-year fight against the SEC, framing it as regulatory overreach / fair-notice failure); Stuart Alderoty (Ripple Chief Legal Officer, who directed the legal defense that won the partial 2023 programmatic-sales ruling and later helped lead the National Cryptocurrency Association industry body); and the broader 'fair notice / major questions doctrine' argument that agencies expanded jurisdiction without Congressional authorization. Courts partially agreed (the Ripple programmatic-sales ruling; Grayscale v. SEC on the spot-ETF denial being 'arbitrary and capricious'). grade: fact (the roles, filings + the rulings); 'who was right' not adjudicated.
the_honest_reading: Map the positions, not the motives. There WAS real fraud (FTX) that warranted prosecution; there WAS contested overreach (developer liability for privacy code; regulation-by-enforcement without rulemaking; a carve-out authored by an official with a documented appearance-of-conflict); and there WAS a wholesale political reversal that flipped the same conduct from illegal to fine. Assigning corrupt INTENT to any individual is out of scope and unsupported — the analysis stops at who held which office, what action they took, and how that action grades on a reasonable<->overreach spectrum. The pendulum itself — enforcement and de-enforcement both tracking the administration rather than a stable rule of law — is the finding. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.
